title: AI & Data Landscape & Tech-Lead Mindset
pitchHtml: Before you can build a pole or sell an offer, you need a map of the territory and a way of thinking that keeps you out of trouble. This module gives both: what "AI & Data" actually contains, who does what, and the single decision you'll make more than any other — should we even use ML/AI here, and if so, the cheap version or the expensive one? The recurring question of this whole path starts here: does the value justify the complexity?

items.html: Draw the AI & Data landscape from raw data to agents, and place any client request on it
Name the roles in an AI & Data pole and what each is (and isn't) accountable for
Decide between rules, classical ML, deep learning, a hosted LLM API, or "don't build it" for a given problem
Separate 2026 reality from hype when a stakeholder asks "can't AI just do this?"
Apply the value/complexity budget to qualify or kill an opportunity in one conversation
Explain why "no usable data" is a stop condition, and what to sell instead

blocks.html: "AI & Data" is a stack, not a single thing. Most client confusion (and most mis-scoped missions) comes from collapsing these layers.

blocks.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
   VALUE / DELIVERY   │ Decisions · Products · Automations        │
                      └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                       ▲
   AI METHODS         Agents → LLM apps → Generative AI → Deep Learning → Classical ML
                                       ▲
   ANALYTICS          BI · dashboards · metrics · experimentation
                                       ▲
   DATA PLATFORM      Pipelines · warehouse/lakehouse · governance · quality
                                       ▲
   SOURCES            Apps · logs · events · documents · 3rd-party APIs

blocks.html:
Two rules of thumb that will save you repeatedly:

  • Value flows top-down, but capability is built bottom-up. Clients ask for the top box ("an AI assistant"). Delivery almost always blocks on the bottom two (no clean data, no platform). Most "AI projects" are 70% data work.
  • Higher in the AI-methods row ≠ better. A regex or a SQL rule that solves the problem beats a fine-tuned model that solves it slightly better at 50× the cost and risk.
You don't need all of these on day one, but you need to know which hat a task wants — and to resist the common trap of asking one person to wear all of them.

RoleOwnsTypical deliverableDon't confuse with
Data EngineerPipelines, ingestion, transformationReliable tables in the warehouseData Scientist
Analytics EngineerModelled, documented data (e.g. dbt)Trusted metrics layerData Engineer
Data Analyst / BIQuestions answered from dataDashboards, analysesData Scientist
Data ScientistModelling, experimentation, inferenceA validated model + insightML Engineer
ML EngineerProductionising & serving modelsA monitored model behind an APIData Scientist
MLOps / PlatformThe infra ML runs onCI/CD, registry, monitoringDevOps
AI EngineerLLM/agent applicationsA shipped GenAI featureData Scientist
Data ArchitectPlatform & governance designTarget architecture, standardsData Engineer
Tech Lead (you)Outcomes, scoping, judgment, the offerThe right thing built, sold, and ledAny single role above

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Staffing a mission is a coverage problem, not a headcount problem. A two-person team where one covers data+platform and the other covers modelling+app can deliver most early missions; the failure mode is hiring four narrow specialists for a job that needed two generalists, or one generalist for a job that needed a real data engineer.

subsections.title: The Decision You'll Make Most: Rules vs ML vs DL vs LLM vs Buy vs Don't

blocks.html: This is the heart of the tech-lead mindset. Walk it top to bottom and stop at the first row that clears the bar.

OptionReach for it whenCost & riskWatch out for
Don't buildThe problem is rare, low-value, or solvable by a process changeLowestThe hardest sell internally; often the right call
Rules / heuristics / SQLLogic is known, stable, explainable; few edge casesVery lowRule sprawl when logic actually is fuzzy
Buy an API / SaaSCapability is a commodity (OCR, transcription, generic chat, translation)Low build, recurring run costLock-in, data residency, per-call cost at scale
Classical MLYou have tabular/structured data + labels; need explainability & cheap inferenceLow-medium"No labels" quietly kills it
Deep learningUnstructured data (images, audio, raw text) and enough of itMedium-high (data, compute, talent)Over-engineering tabular problems
Hosted LLMLanguage understanding/generation, low-medium volume, fuzzy tasksLow build, variable run costHallucination, latency, per-token cost, privacy
Fine-tune / self-host LLMHigh volume, strict privacy, or a durable specialised needHighRarely justified early — see Large Language Models in Practice
The default escalation: rule → buy → classical ML → deep learning / LLM. Make stakeholders justify each step up, not each step down.

What to tell a stakeholder who says "AI can surely just do this":

  • Genuinely strong now: language tasks (drafting, summarising, extraction, classification), code assistance, retrieval over your documents (RAG), perception (vision/speech), and well-posed tabular prediction. These ship.
  • Real but fragile: multi-step agents that take actions — powerful demos, harder production reliability; scope tightly and add guardrails (see Agentic Architectures & LLM App Patterns).
  • Oversold: "autonomous" anything with no human in the loop; "we'll just fine-tune it" as a first move; AI that compensates for absent or terrible data; instant ROI with no change management.
  • The quiet truth: most realised value still comes from boring wins — a clean metric, an automated report, a good classifier, a search that works — not from the frontier. Sell the boring wins; they fund the ambitious ones.

AI is a function of data. Before any modelling promise, you must be able to answer: does the data exist, can we access it lawfully, is it usable, and is there enough of it?

  • No data → no AI. If the signal isn't recorded anywhere, the first mission is instrumentation/collection, not modelling. That's a legitimate (and sellable) data-engineering engagement, not a failure.
  • "Garbage in, garbage out" is a budget line. Assume the majority of a first mission is data acquisition, cleaning, and access — price and plan for it (see Working with Data: Sources, Quality & Feature Engineering and Data Engineering & Platforms).
  • Lawful basis is a gate, not a footnote. If the data is personal, governance is a prerequisite, not a final-week checkbox (see Data Governance, Privacy & Pseudonymisation).

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Think of an opportunity portfolio in expected-value terms: EV≈P(success)×value−costEV \approx P(\text{success}) \times \text{value} - \text{cost}EV≈P(success)×value−cost. Frontier projects have huge value but low P(success)P(\text{success})P(success) and high cost; "boring" data/automation wins have modest value but high P(success)P(\text{success})P(success) and low cost. A healthy pole runs a portfolio weighted toward the latter, funding a few of the former. No derivation needed — the point is to make the three terms explicit before committing.

The landscape module sells the entry-point missions — the ones that qualify a client and open the door to everything else.

  • AI & Data Readiness Assessment — Problem: client wants AI but doesn't know if they can. Deliverable: a scored assessment of data, platform, skills, and governance maturity + a prioritised gap list. Scope: 2-4 weeks, fixed price. Who buys: CTO/CDO, COMEX wanting a reality check.
  • Use-Case Discovery & Prioritisation — Problem: "we should do something with AI." Deliverable: a backlog of use cases scored on value × feasibility (a 2×2), with 2-3 recommended starters. Scope: 2-3 weeks. Who buys: business unit leaders, innovation/digital teams.
  • AI Strategy & Roadmap — Problem: scattered experiments, no direction. Deliverable: target operating model, build-vs-buy stance, 6-12 month roadmap, budget envelope. Scope: 4-6 weeks. Who buys: executive sponsor. (Assembled in Building the AI & Data Offer & Leading the Pole.)

Buy-vs-build for the client at this stage: sell judgment and direction, not technology. The deliverable is a decision, de-risked. What to charge for: the assessment/roadmap as a fixed-price package — it's repeatable IP. What not to promise: specific accuracy numbers or ROI before you've seen the data; commit only to a method for finding them.
As of 2026 — treat names/prices as perishable; re-check before quoting.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote
Cloud AI suites (AWS / Azure / GCP)One-stop platform for a polePaid (consumption)Convenient; watch lock-in & egress
Hosted LLM APIs (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI, Google, Mistral)Commodity language capabilityPaid (per-token)Compare on price, latency, data terms
Maturity/landscape frameworks (Gartner, internal scorecards)Readiness assessmentsMixedUse as a checklist, not gospel
Whiteboard / Miro / FigJamUse-case mapping workshopsFreemiumThe actual tool of this module is a workshop
The deliverables of this module are mostly conversations and documents — the heavy tooling starts in Part II onward.

SituationAI/ML is overkill — do this instead
Logic is known and stableWrite the rules; ship in days
The decision happens 10×/monthLet a human do it; automate the data prep around them
No labelled data and no way to get itSell a data-collection/instrumentation mission first
A €30/month SaaS already does itBuy it; integrate; move on
The real problem is a broken processFix the process; AI won't paper over it
The most senior thing you can say in a sales meeting is "you don't need AI for this." It builds the trust that wins the missions where they do.

• Agrawal, Gans & Goldfarb — Prediction Machines — the economics of AI, in business terms.
  • Chip Huyen — AI Engineering — pragmatic framing of the modern stack. (Paid)

• Annual state-of-AI / model-pricing pages (a16z, vendor pricing) — for the current cost landscape. Date everything.
  • CNIL and EU resources on AI — for the European reality (ties into Data Governance, Privacy & Pseudonymisation).

• Maths behind any method mentioned here → Index AIS.
  • Production/engineering reality of shipping one model → Index MLE.

subsections.title: Decision Checklist — "Should we use AI here?"

items.html: Is there a clear business outcome with a number attached?
Does the data exist, and can we access it lawfully?
Would a rule or an off-the-shelf API clear the bar more cheaply?
Can we tolerate the failure mode (wrong prediction / hallucination)?
Is there a human-in-the-loop path if confidence is low?
Do we know how we'll prove it worked?

blocks.html:
• "We'll figure out the data later." (You won't; it's the project.)
  • "We just need to fine-tune a model." (As a first move — almost never.)
  • "It worked in the demo." (Demos hide the long tail.)
  • A use case with no owner who feels the pain.
  • Success defined as "use AI", not as an outcome.

• "If this worked perfectly, what decision or action changes, and what is that worth?"
  • "Where does the data live today, who owns it, and what's in it?"
  • "What do you do today without AI, and what's wrong with that?"
  • "Who is accountable for acting on the output?"
  • "What happens when the system is wrong?"
Before moving on, you should be able to:

items.html: Sketch the AI & Data stack and locate any incoming request on it
Route a problem to rules / buy / classical ML / DL / LLM / don't-build with a one-line justification
Defuse an inflated AI expectation with a concrete 2026 reality check
Run a value/complexity conversation that ends in qualify, downscope, or kill
Recognise a "no usable data" situation and pivot it into a data mission
Pitch a readiness assessment or use-case discovery as a fixed-price entry mission
